questions.question: Explain the three steps involved in Divide and Conquer approach with an example.
keyPoints: Divide: Break problem into smaller subproblems
Conquer: Solve subproblems recursively
Combine: Merge solutions of subproblems
Example: Merge Sort divides array, sorts halves, merges sorted halves

questions.question: State and explain the Master Theorem. Also solve T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n using Master Theorem.
keyPoints: Master Theorem solves T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)
Three cases based on comparison of f(n) with n^logb(a)
For T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n: a=2, b=2, n^logb(a)=n
Since f(n) = Θ(n^logb(a)), Case 2 applies: T(n) = Θ(n log n)

questions.question: Compare Merge Sort and Quick Sort with respect to time complexity, space complexity, and stability.
keyPoints: Time: Both O(n log n) average; Quick Sort O(n²) worst case
Space: Merge Sort O(n), Quick Sort O(log n)
Stability: Merge Sort is stable, Quick Sort is not stable
In-place: Quick Sort in-place, Merge Sort not in-place

questions.question: Write the recurrence relation for Merge Sort and solve it using recurrence tree method.
keyPoints: At each level, work done is n
Number of levels = log n
Total work = n × log n = O(n log n)

questions.question: What is theClosest Pair of Points problem? State its time complexity using Divide and Conquer.
keyPoints: Find pair of points with minimum Euclidean distance in 2D
Divide: Sort points by x-coordinate, split vertically
Conquer: Recursively find minimum in left and right halves
Combine: Check strip for closer pairs across divide
Time complexity: O(n log n)

questions.question: Explain why Quick Sort is preferred over Merge Sort for arrays in practice despite both having O(n log n) average time.
keyPoints: Quick Sort is in-place requiring O(1) extra space vs O(n) for Merge Sort
Better cache performance due to sequential access patterns
Overhead of copying data in Merge Sort
Quick Sort typically has smaller constant factors

questions.question: Write the algorithm for Binary Search using Divide and Conquer. What is its time complexity?
keyPoints: Divide: Find mid element
Conquer: Recursively search left or right half based on comparison
Combine: No combine step needed
Time complexity: O(log n)

questions.question: Write a detailed note on Divide and Conquer technique. How does it differ from other algorithm design techniques? Discuss with examples.
keyPoints: Definition: Breaking problem into smaller subproblems, solving recursively, combining results
Steps: Divide, Conquer, Combine
Examples: Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Binary Search, Strassen's Matrix Multiplication
Difference from Dynamic Programming: D&C has independent subproblems, DP has overlapping
Difference from Greedy: Greedy makes locally optimal choice, D&C explores all possibilities
Advantages: Reduces complexity, parallelizable, elegant solution
Disadvantages: Stack overflow, overhead of recursion

questions.question: Explain the Quick Sort algorithm with suitable example. Discuss various pivot selection strategies and analyze its time complexity in best, average, and worst cases.
keyPoints: Algorithm: Select pivot, partition array around pivot, recursively sort subarrays
Pivot strategies: First element, last element, random, median-of-three
Best case: O(n log n) - balanced partitions
Average case: O(n log n)
Worst case: O(n²) - unbalanced partitions (sorted array with first/last pivot)
Partitioning: One traversal placing elements less than pivot on left, greater on right
Space: O(log n) average, O(n) worst for recursion stack

questions.question: Explain Merge Sort with a suitable example. Derive its time complexity using recurrence relation. Why is it considered stable?
keyPoints: Algorithm: Divide array into halves, recursively sort, merge sorted halves
Example: [38, 27, 43, 3, 9] → split → sort → merge
Recurrence: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n)
Solution: O(n log n) using Master Theorem or recursion tree
Stable: Equal elements maintain relative order during merge
External sorting: Suitable for sorting large files due to sequential access

questions.question:
Solve the following recurrences using Master Theorem:
(i) T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n²
(ii) T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n
(iii) T(n) = 3T(n/2) + n²
keyPoints: (i) a=2, b=2, n^logb(a)=n, f(n)=n²: f(n) dominates → Ω(n^logb(a+ε)) → T(n)=Θ(n²)
(ii) a=4, b=2, n^logb(a)=n², f(n)=n: f(n) is smaller → O(n^logb(a-ε)) → T(n)=Θ(n²)
(iii) a=3, b=2, n^log_2(3)≈n^1.585, f(n)=n²: f(n) dominates → T(n)=Θ(n²)
